scenarios.task: I'm the CEO of a B2B SaaS company (~$18M ARR). Great quarter: bookings up 40% YoY and sales wants bigger accelerators. Our controller mentioned deferred revenue was roughly flat this quarter and DSO moved from 48 to 61 days, but revenue recognized grew 12%. I want to raise next year's growth targets on the back of this. What's your read?
criteria: Flags that flat deferred revenue contradicts strong prepaid-SaaS bookings growth and demands a bookings-to-billings-to-deferred reconciliation before trusting the 40%
Treats the DSO move from 48 to 61 days (>15% jump) as a specific red flag suggesting collections slippage or side-letter/extended terms used to book deals
Distinguishes bookings from billings from recognized revenue from ARR rather than treating them as one growth number
Asks to see AR aging and/or non-standard payment terms granted recently before endorsing higher targets
Does not simply congratulate and proceed to raising targets

scenarios.task: We're a 110-person SaaS company, $8.5M in the bank, net burn about $350K/month. Our bank offered a $10M term loan at SOFR+3.5% with a minimum-liquidity covenant and a fixed-charge coverage covenant. CFO hat on: should we take it? What would you check first?
criteria: Models covenant compliance on forward projections (including a bear case), not just current actuals
Identifies that the binding constraint is usually one specific covenant (e.g. minimum liquidity) and quantifies headroom against it
Considers structuring alternatives such as a smaller initial draw plus a delayed-draw tranche tied to milestones
Notes that debt extends runway only if the covenants don't effectively hand the bank control in the downside scenario where the money is actually needed
Asks about covenant cure/waiver mechanics or what happens on breach, not just the rate

scenarios.task: Board meeting in two weeks. Our gross margin slid from 78% to 75.5% over the last two quarters. Nobody changed pricing. Head of engineering says it's 'just AWS costs from growth.' How should I get to the bottom of this and what do I tell the board?
criteria: Treats a >200bps gross margin slide without pricing change as a diagnosable anomaly, not an acceptable growth cost by default
Decomposes COGS into specific candidate causes (infrastructure unit costs, support headcount classification, customer mix shift) rather than accepting one narrative
Checks whether costs are growing faster than the revenue they serve (unit economics per customer or per workload), not just in absolute terms
Proposes telling the board the diagnosis and trajectory with numbers, not smoothing over the decline
Asks whether anything was reclassified into or out of COGS recently
